narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile shows determination to pursue this path, but the admissions model focused on key indicators from past students' paths to the bar exam. The model predicts a 63% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish average, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in law school might struggle compared to peers. This outweighed your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, which is close to the average of 3.21 and actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward bar exam success. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also below the average around 5.7, added further concern, reinforcing a pattern of lower rankings that the model links to higher bar failure risk. Other elements like your lower-middle family income bracket and LSAT score of 28—below the usual 36.7—played roles but were less decisive here. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, you're in the lower deciles early on, which stands out as a red flag even if your undergrad record holds up decently. Full-time status aligns with most applicants at 93%, so it didn't sway things much. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or your background—it's the model highlighting where your academics dipped relative to bar passers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score significantly above average, strengthening your early decile performance through targeted study habits, or exploring programs that support students from similar income backgrounds. Building a stronger narrative around your Year 1 and 3 rankings could also help in reapplying or appealing. With focused improvements, you can shift these odds in your favor next time.
